pattern: Two-letter words only ever help a palindrome by matching against their own mirror-image word, so counting complementary pairs (and one leftover self-mirrored word for the center) is enough.
hints: Only two kinds of words can ever sit symmetrically in the final palindrome: a word paired with its exact reverse on the opposite side, or a word that is its own reverse.
For every word that isn't its own reverse, count how many complete pairs you can form with its reverse elsewhere in the list -- each such pair contributes four characters to the total length.
For words that are their own reverse, pairs of two also contribute four characters each, and at most one leftover single copy of such a word may sit alone in the very center, contributing two more.

edge_inputs.description: one complementary pair plus one self-mirrored word for the center
two complementary pairs among unrelated words, none self-mirrored
three different self-mirrored words but only one may become the center
no word has its reverse present anywhere, answer is zero
single word that is not its own reverse, cannot form anything
single self-mirrored word, becomes the center alone
the same non-self-mirrored word repeated with no reverse ever appearing
two different self-mirrored words each with an odd leftover, only one center allowed
many copies of one complementary pair

stress.description: random two-letter lowercase words drawn from the full alphabet, at large scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    letters = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    words = []
    for _ in range(n):
        a = rng.choice(letters)
        b = rng.choice(letters)
        words.append(a + b)
    return [words]
stress.note: drawing both letters from the full alphabet keeps a healthy mix of complementary pairs and self-mirrored words at scale
